Autopilot Systems.clue: Servos move these three surfaces: the aileron, elevator, and rudder.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Ailerons, elevator, and rudder are the primary flight controls driven by autopilot servos.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Flaps, slats, and spoilers fall into this category of flight control.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Lift and drag devices such as flaps, slats, and spoilers are secondary or auxiliary flight controls.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Post-pitch-servo-maintenance nose bob with this name, caused by bridle tension error.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Porpoising after pitch servo work is the bridle-tension symptom.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This computer paints pitch and roll command bars on the attitude indicator.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: The flight director computer draws the command bars.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This autopilot subsystem damps vertical-axis oscillation.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: The yaw damper suppresses yaw-axis oscillation.

Autopilot Systems.clue: These three control surfaces are moved by a 3-axis autopilot.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Three-axis means pitch, roll, and yaw.

Autopilot Systems.clue: The abbreviation IAS on an autopilot mode annunciator stands for this.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: IAS = Indicated Airspeed.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Rotating the heading bug while altitude hold is engaged has this effect on vertical mode.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Lateral and vertical modes are decoupled.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This control system continuously compares output to command and corrects the error.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: A closed-loop feedback system uses error correction.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This sensor/unit provides pitch and roll attitude to the autopilot.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Attitude reference comes from the vertical gyro or AHRS.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This coupled yaw-roll oscillation in swept-wing jets is what the yaw damper targets.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Dutch roll is the classic yaw/roll oscillation.

Autopilot Systems.clue: The yaw damper uses this sensor input to fight Dutch roll.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Yaw-rate input drives the yaw damper's rudder command.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This is what a pitch servo does when commanded: apply force to this surface.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Pitch servo is an actuator that moves the elevator.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This mode keeps the wings level without pilot wheel input.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Attitude hold maintains the roll attitude existing at engagement.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Preflight autopilot self-test exercises these two items.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: The self-test exercises the servos and the annunciator light sequence.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This computer feeds barometric altitude data to the autopilot.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: The ADC produces altitude data for altitude hold.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Autopilot pitch trim does this during extended climbs or descents.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Auto-trim offloads the sustained elevator force from the servo during extended climbs or descents, preventing saturation.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Pitch trim disconnects automatically when trim runs past one of these limits.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Runaway-trim protection uses time and position thresholds.

Autopilot Systems.clue: This is the core difference between FD and autopilot.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: The flight director displays command bars for the pilot to follow; the autopilot actually moves the flight controls through the servos.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Pitch axis won't respond — most likely this is the culprit.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: A servo clutch jam or loss of position feedback is the classic cause of an unresponsive pitch axis.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Instant autopilot disconnect on engagement points to this.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Sensor mismatch fails the engagement check and aborts.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Ceaseless left-right roll in heading mode likely means this.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Persistent left-right oscillation in heading mode is classic excessive roll gain or a bad feedback signal.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Missing FD command bars — verify these inputs first.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Without AHRS/ADC inputs, the FD cannot draw its bars.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Unstable roll/yaw with heading wander points to this degraded signal.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: A degraded yaw rate or heading reference produces the unstable roll/yaw and heading wander symptoms.

Autopilot Systems.clue: Altitude-hold hunting during descent — likely this fault.
Autopilot Systems.explanation: Capture instability during descent is caused by pitch gain error or a bad altitude input to the pitch loop.
